queryStrategy: Back to the blog's core niche (52 AI posts). Two big shifts happened since the 26 May career research: (1) Claude Opus 4.8 shipped 28 May 2026 with dynamic workflows / hundreds of parallel subagents in Claude Code — nothing on the blog covers it yet; (2) the agentic-coding cost crisis went mainstream (pipelines burning 62M tokens/day, $2,500 budgets drained in 24h, AI bill becoming the #2 engineering line item). Also surfacing: the four-tool convergence (Claude Code/Cursor/Codex/Antigravity 'six months in'), new subquadratic & open-weight coding models (SubQ 12M ctx, MiniMax M3, Qwen3 Coder Next), MCP's jump to the Linux Foundation, and a growing 'AI coding is getting worse / uncomfortable truths' backlash. Avoided duplicates: MCP-vs-A2A, Antigravity hands-on, GPT-5.3-Codex, Cursor Composer 2.5 are already published.

topics.title: Claude Opus 4.8 Dynamic Workflows — Spawning Hundreds of Parallel Subagents in Claude Code (Hands-On)
topics.whyTrending: Opus 4.8 shipped 28 May 2026 with a Claude Code research preview that lets one orchestrator session spawn up to 1,000 parallel subagents, each with its own context window, then verify against the repo's own test suite. It's the single biggest agentic-coding capability jump of the year and the blog has zero coverage. Huge fresh search intent, perfect for a code-heavy hands-on with a real repo-scale migration example.

topics.title: I Burned 62 Million Tokens in a Day — The 2026 Agentic Coding Cost Crisis and How to Survive It
topics.whyTrending: Widely-shared report of a pipeline burning 62M tokens in 24h and draining a $2,500/mo budget in one day. Average agentic dev now spends $400-$1,500/mo, extreme cases $4k+ in days; AI is becoming the #2 engineering cost after salaries. Highly emotional, controversial, and practical — readers are actively searching for damage control. Pairs hard data with an opinionated survival playbook.

topics.title: Context Engineering for Coding Agents — 9 Levers That Cut Token Bills 50-70% in Two Weeks
topics.whyTrending: Anthropic's Applied AI team formalized 'context engineering' and after 30 audits found four levers (budget caps, prompt caching, model-tier routing, context pruning) that cut agent costs 50-70% within two weeks — one SaaS went $87k→$24k month-over-month. Concrete, code-able, low-competition practical content with strong commercial intent. Natural companion to the token-burn piece.

topics.title: Six Months In — How Claude Code, Cursor, Codex and Antigravity Quietly Converged on One Blueprint
topics.whyTrending: By June 2026 the 'what should an agentic tool be' debate is largely over: four products converged on a common shape, splitting into IDE copilots, terminal agents, and autonomous background workers. The New Stack's 'six months in' retrospective is fresh. Strong evergreen+timely comparison; the blog already has individual tool posts but no convergence/state-of-the-market synthesis with a real multi-tool workflow.

topics.title: SubQ and the 12M-Token Coding Agent — Is Subquadratic Attention the End of RAG-Style Chunking?
topics.whyTrending: SubQ shipped the first commercial subquadratic LLM with native 12M-token context plus SubQ Code, a repo-wide coding agent built to use the whole context at once. If repo-scale context becomes cheap, the chunking/RAG playbook for code changes fundamentally. Novel architecture angle, almost no practical developer-facing coverage yet, sparks debate.

topics.title: Open-Weight Catches Up — MiniMax M3 and Qwen3 Coder Next vs the Closed Coding Frontier
topics.whyTrending: MiniMax M3 (June 2026) is the first open-weight model to combine frontier coding, 1M context and native multimodality, topping open-weight SWE-Bench Pro at 59.0%; Qwen3 Coder Next released 6 June with 256K context. Self-hosting / cost-control crowd is hungry for a 'can I ditch the closed APIs yet?' benchmark-driven verdict. High commercial intent, ties into the cost-crisis narrative.

topics.title: The Uncomfortable Truths About AI Coding Agents Nobody Puts in the Demo
topics.whyTrending: HN threads 'Some uncomfortable truths about AI coding agents' and 'AI coding assistants are getting worse?' are driving a real backlash conversation in mid-2026: stuck loops burning 30k-75k tokens on unsolvable problems, maintenance-cost blowup, the job getting less fun. Opinionated counter-narrative against the hype — high engagement, shareable, differentiates from vendor-friendly content.

topics.title: MCP Grew Up — Linux Foundation, 97M Downloads, and What MCP Apps + Streaming Mean for Your Agents
topics.whyTrending: MCP crossed 97M monthly SDK downloads, moved under the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation in ~14 months, and the 2026 roadmap adds MCP Apps (renderable UI), streaming resources, built-in OAuth, and first-class remote servers. The blog has a draft MCP-vs-A2A piece but nothing on this maturity leap. Practical 'what to build now' angle for tool authors.

topics.title: Multi-Day Automations — Putting Codex's GPT-5.5 Background Agent to Work Without It Going Rogue
topics.whyTrending: Codex's headline 2026 feature is multi-day automations: GPT-5.5 jobs that run for hours or days unsupervised across cloud, IDE, browser and CLI — the 'autonomous background worker' category. Devs are curious but nervous about cost and safety. Practical guardrails + a concrete long-running job walkthrough is wide-open content with strong intent.

topics.title: AI Agents Are Eating SaaS — What 56% of Engineering Work Being AI-Done Means for the Tools You Buy
topics.whyTrending: JetBrains/Anthropic 2026 data: 95% use AI weekly, 56% report 70%+ of engineering work done with AI, and the HN-trending 'AI agents are starting to eat SaaS' thesis says agents are collapsing point-solution tools. Big-picture, opinion-driven piece on how the build-vs-buy calculus and the SaaS stack shift when an agent can just do it. Sparks discussion, strong evergreen value.
